text:
### *Editor • Publisher*

Tom Luongo is a former research chemist and part-time goat farmer. He's always been a writer. He is also Editor of *Ultimate Wealth Report* and contributor to *Financial Intelligence Report* for Newsmax Media.

The publisher of the *Gold, Goats 'n Guns* blog and podcast, his work is also published in *Bitcoin Magazine* and syndicated regularly at sites such as ZeroHedge, Lewrockwell.com and Investing.com.

An advanced student of traditional Tae Kwon Do and an adherent to Austrian economics, he applies those lessons to geopolitics, gold and central bank policy. He lives on his hobby farm in North Florida with his wife, daughter, 5 dogs, 10 goats, 15 ducks and a cat.

heading_path: Tom Luongo In Chaos, We Trust
text:
## Tom Luongo In Chaos, We Trust

![](_page_0_Picture_8.jpeg)

I grew up in upstate New York in and around where the Revolutionary War was fought. The woods that surrounded my neighborhood growing up were littered with old rock walls, relics of a previous age. We lived in fear of uncovering a nest of ground wasps or hornets while trampling around during summers off from school.

You learned to avoid the obvious ones because there's little worse than getting stung a few dozen times by angry flying insects. When they invade your yard, however, you either cede

territory or attack without mercy, scattering the survivors so we could live for a while in peace. But that moment when you decide to take them on is pretty frickin' tense.

There's no negotiating with a nest of hornets. It's either you or them.

Donald Trump is a guy that likes knocking over hornet's nests. He's been kicking over a lot of them this year. This issue explores what happens afterwards.

*— Tom*

![](_page_0_Picture_15.jpeg)

## Quote of the month:

 *"Brrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrt!"* 

 *— A-10 Thunderbolt II "Warthog"*

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text: ## THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text:
### WEIGHING TRUMP'S IRAN GAMBLE

![](_page_1_Picture_2.jpeg)

*Just over two years ago,* I did [a podcast](https://www.spreaker.com/episode/episode-168-pete-quinones-and-the-plight-of-being-post-libertarian--58548416) with Pete Quiñones where two newly self-described "post-libertarians" discussed the end of "consensus politics" and the rise of power politics.

It was an important conversation for me as it helped clarify in real time what I was feeling but struggling to put into words. I could feel the world shifting and my interpretation of how I interpreted that shift was also changing.

In hindsight, two-plus years of making a strong case for the Federal Reserve's shift in monetary policy had worn down my resistance to seeing the potential positive effects of power politics. Libertarian thought is fundamentally hostile to all forms of this type of thinking, and short circuits when it is successful.

The Fed's policy was sustainable because of the downstream effects of removing LIBOR from dollar pricing, after moving the US to SOFR.

Powell's "higher for longer" rate policy was power politics at its highest level. It used the pricing of global dollar flows to enforce US sovereignty over its economic destiny. This seems like an obvious thing for the US central bank to do, but like many things in geopolitics today, there are many actors acting against their country's best interests.

And it became impossible for me to not at least acknowledge the value of that approach.

What started as me issuing *mea culpas* such as, "I'm not saying I like the Fed or anything, but this is what I'm seeing," turned quickly into me posting memes of Jerome Powell as Don Corleone stroking his cat.

Consensus politics is as much as lie as anything we've ever been presented with. It was always power politics, but it was given the veneer of inclusiveness, debate, and compromise to sell it to the normies.

For me, this lie became obvious for the first the time during the Brexit negotiations. The European Union (EU) was definitely not negotiating with the UK over their exit from the union, they were simply dressing up their butthurt and intransigence with

endless angst over niggling issues to distract from core positions.

Consensus politics is a lawyer's game, built on words, definitions of what "is" is, and referrals to committees. In other words, it's all just gaslighting.

People have been conditioned to expect the endless debate and wrangling to get even the most mundane task approved by those committees. When someone comes along and refuses to accept the validity of that framework, there is a wholly predictable response of shock and dismay.

But, gentlemen, you can, in fact, fight in the war room. And Donald Trump revels in that.

This is what happened when Powell raised rates quickly in 2022. He didn't consult the other G-7 central bankers. He didn't ask permission. He was the FOMC Chair, saw the situation in the US for what it was (dire) and tested the argument that the US economy was more capable of absorbing his rate shock than everyone else.

He was correct.

This is why I was able to then begin the long pivot away from a purely Austrian economics interpretation of the global capital markets and begin seeing the limitations of individualist politics in the context of the world we actually lived in.

Since the beginnings of *Gold, Goats 'n Guns* I've said that I would interpret events based on "the world I've got, not the world I want."

Never was this more apparent than during those early days of trying to interpret what the Fed's goals were.

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text:
### *Discomfort Junction*

That shift in thinking was very uncomfortable, and it alienated a lot of people while attracting others. Since then, as this period of crisis and war has intensified, and is downstream of Powell's policy shift at the Fed, it's become increasingly obvious that the illusions of consensus are gone and only power politics matters.

Many people haven't been able to process this, having become very accustomed to that illusion. This is very true for a lot of commentators honestly trying to figure out what is happening.

Since 2026 began however, President Trump has been nothing short of a power politics juggernaut, for better or worse. And, as always, the confusion and reactions to his moves have been illuminating.

He started with nabbing Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in January, then he took out CJNG cartel leader El Mencho in February. Now, this month he's embroiled the US, and by extension, the world in a big fight to finish off his long-term project of ending one of the biggest global threats to peace, the dismantling the Islamic Republic of Iran's Axis of Resistance.

And it's that illumination I want to focus on this month. The goal this month isn't advocacy or opposition to Trump's actions. I've made my peace with this and support where he has gone.

As an observer and commentator on this subject, I've been, personally, on both sides of this fence over the years, being against (and aghast) at Trump's assassination of IRGC General Qassem Soleimani in 2020 and today sick to death of the IRGC's game of pursuing a nuclear weapon.

My opinion on this is irrelevant. Power politics doesn't care about your feelings. Nations have interests and leaders should always pursue those interests to the best of their ability. When you see someone acting or negotiating against his nation's best interests, however, you should question who it is they actually work for.

That statement sits at the heart of my analysis at this point in the game. It is inferential and differential, and it may, at times, look like I have lost my mind (as Dexter has more than once reminded me of), but in the

*"Since 2026 began…President Trump has been nothing short of a power politics juggernaut, for better or worse. And, as always, the confusion and reactions to his moves have been illuminating."*

world of power politics, people intentionally masking their true motivations is absolutely part of the landscape leaving us having to fit pieces into weird holes.

And because of this I am going to state one of my operating assumptions, which is that the Islamic Republic of Iran is a network node of *Davos* and that their pursuit of nuclear weapons advances both groups' ambitions.

This is why when national interests come into conflict they will be resolved, one way or another. Trump has acted. He unleashed US military and kicked the living hell out of the IRGC. There is no argument about that. Anyone still making an argument about Iranian potency now bears the burden of proof.

But that is only the first part of this. And now we must assess the situation as best as we can with the information we have.

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text:
### *Bad News First*

For months we've had to navigate every decision Trump makes based on a single framework, can he carry the midterm elections this November? The thinking is clear-headed and straightforward.

Given that *Davos* and his other myriad enemies would like nothing more than for the global architecture and flow of money and power to return to the pre-Trump *status quo*, they will do everything possible to queer the midterms, impeach him, end MAGA, and run the table on the US.

The most hyperbolic of these projections have Trump and his vice-president J.D. Vance impeached and convicted on day one and installing Dollar Store Obama, Hakeem Jeffries, as first Speaker of the House and then as the 48th (and last) president of the US.

I don't rule out that possibility, certainly. But when the stakes to the average person who voted for Trump in 2024 are that high, the idea that they are going to put the crazies back in charge who still think you should be stoned for not taking an experimental gene therapy or can't define what a woman is, is equally improbable.

So, in the context of this will Trump's having cleaved the Iranian Gordian knot in a way that has unnerved a lot of people leave him vulnerable at the midterm elections next fall?

If nothing changes between now and then, yes. No question. The problem with that is there are nearly eight months between now and election day. And there is a lot of change we should expect between now and then.

Looking back to just the beginning of the year, things have changed radically.

Most of the people in the so-called "True MAGA" camp around Steve Bannon have thoroughly discredited themselves after

> staking out positions which are untenable in light of the US military's performance so far this month. Be they antiwar absolutists or legal experts of dubious distinction, their conclusion is the same.

Trump has betrayed MAGA and no one should vote for him this fall.

The classic neocons like Bill Kristol and John Bolton who have always hated Trump are angry because they know Trump isn't interested in their version of regime change: Iran becomes a failed state and a constant source of chaos for a generation. The new "Iran" will be hailed a "democracy" because they have a puppet civilian government.

This is consistent with why the UK refused to go along with any of Trump's plans as Keir Starmer lamely cited it as an 'illegal war.' Most of Europe refused to be a part of it as well. They fully declined to support him policing the Straits of Hormuz which they rely on, calling into question NATO's future openly.

Again, it looks like Iran to *Davos* is just another piece on their gameboard with their real target being the US.

And again, no one should vote for this crazy man in the White House that refuses to play by their consensus rules.

The entire part of the alt-media set who believed Iran and Russia formed some kind of true resistance node to US hegemony have been revealed as clueless as well. Because whether you're like me and see the dying empires of Europe as the immediate threat by going out in a blaze of glory, or like Dexter who sees China as the empire over the near horizon willing to use Europe and Iran for their purposes, the US defending its interests is not the problem here unless you literally eat crayons.

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text:
### *(Warmaking, con't.)*

There have been endless statements from "trusted" former US military and intelligence officers spouting absolute lies about the damage Iran caused and minimized the devastation meted out on Iran. They proved themselves to be incapable of distinguishing fantasy from reality or even doing a modicum of strategic analysis.

The single common denominator in all these insane takes is emotion, be it anger, spite, envy, greed, or fear. They all fail to pass the basic test of something approaching facts.

And yet, those who have been wrong about everything still expect to be taken seriously now. The aftershocks of their crash-outs and obvious psychological operations may convince a few people to stay home this fall, but these will only linger if nothing changes between now and then.

That said, let's look at some real tail risks going into this summer.

As long as the Straits of Hormuz are "closed" the price of oil will be elevated. The main factor in closing the straits to tanker traffic is the canceling of shipping insurance from the British

cartel of insurers, led by Lloyd's

of London.

That announcement was immediately challenged by Trump who announced a plan to provide reinsurance coverage and take some amount of that business from London. Three weeks later, and we're waiting for takers of that business and whether this \$20 billion program will apparently be reinsured through the International Development Finance Corporation with policies written by Chubb Limited (NYSE:CB).

You can expect a lot of armtwisting going on behind the scenes as tankers are stalled waiting for orders. Ship captains and executives are doing the cost/benefit analysis during maximum fog of war with billions at stake and crazy people at the trigger.

Iran, for its part, has capitalized to make it look like they are in control of the situation, saying the straits aren't closed to anyone who isn't an ally of the US or Israel. They say this as every day IRGC and religious leaders are knocked off their perches. The US and Israel don't use the straits, so this is aimed at further dividing European countries from the US, who refuse to assist their NATO ally in unlocking the strait. This makes the case stronger by the day their interests overlap.

Iran is at least honest about wanting to kill America. The Europeans apparently aren't done sucking us dry yet.

That said, to date, the war premium is real. And it will have real consequences on the US and global economy. That economic fallout is already being felt as money is going to ground across the world. The USDX popped over 100 for the first time since November as everyone waits to see what happens next. Gold has stalled right at \$4,500/oz. Silver is treading water below \$75/oz.

Across the world bond yields are up (US 10-year yields have quickly reversed a break below 4%, rising to 4.28% on 3/13) and equity markets have reversed recent breakouts to new highs. Last month I talked about the Dow Jones Industrials breaking 50,000, getting a strong Dow Theory confirmation signal from the Dow Jones Transportation Average. This month I'm wondering if a return to the April 2025 "Tariff Tantrum" lows near 36,600 are on the horizon.

War does that to markets. They are the very definition of uncertainty. And that is a big tail risk to Donald Trump's chances of holding or expanding his slim legislative majority at the midterms.

This is the essence of *Davos'* and Iran's gambit, which I see here as synonymous, can they take the hits to their powerbases long enough to bleed Trump out in November? That's their long game. The traitors in Congress are certainly helping this along by refusing to pass any legislation that would implement what voters want.

Much of this strategy is based on past negotiations as well as

past assumptions about the US political cycle.

Iran and Davos are calculating that Trump has a very low pain threshold because of the midterms. Their demoralization campaign launched from every front in this 5th generation war feeds this. The project to begin splitting the MAGA coalition started within minutes of Thomas Crooks missing Trump at Butler, PA.

More importantly, they believe Trump is a vain and stupid man of low character and lower taste.

And while some of that may be true, it shouldn't be a basis for a long-term strategy to oppose him. Because what if, like he told the press recently, that he isn't doing this for today, worried about the political fallout. He's doing it for the future and that

future cannot have the former leadership of Iran armed with a nuclear weapon.

The bad news all comes down to the status quo in the US and the world resuming. That a decade after the twin shocks of 2016 – Brexit and Trump's first election – we're watching the final showdown of decades of moves and countermoves. This war with Iran will define Trump's success or failure as president.

![](_page_3_Picture_23.jpeg)

While nearly everyone is focused on the down sides for Trump and the US, as they are focused in the moment, the movements in currencies, oil, the ships backing up in ports, etc.

What they are not focusing on is the fundamental changes that are over the horizon regardless of how long Iran and Davos can keep the straits effectively closed. In fact, for Trump, the longer the straits stay closed the more damage his enemies take, and the advantages accrue to the US.

![](_page_3_Picture_26.jpeg)

Most of the oil that flows through the straits goes to China, Japan, Korea, and Europe. Trump is in the process of expanding US oil output. Russia is expanding its arctic sea routes. The Hormuz represents a significant chokepoint that Trump wants to highlight to wrest pricing control over oil away from a third party. Today it's set by London through the combination of the Brent futures contract and the insurance mafia. But don't think that Beijing with its SHFE medium-sour contract or the UAE with its Dubai contract do not also want that business.

Both also run gold exchanges, also an important part of this equation.

What's clear is that Trump wants that business and is willing to start a major war to get it by removing the chaos premium of Iran's regional belligerence with its ready-made enemy Israel from the equation.

My read is that he went into Iran was for all his stated reasons about Iran's nuclear ambitions, but that those ambitions were

the end game in a larger strategy to maintain the current system of control over the physical collateral of global finance, oil and gold prices.

By pushing Iran into "closing the Straits of Hormuz" Trump finally blew up this Gordian knot and invited everyone to feel what real chaos looks like rather than tiptoeing around Iran's everincreasing attempts at blackmailing the world.

So, you want to be considered a major player on the world stage? Fine. Deal with this. And there is no argument about what "this" is, it's the combined might of US logistics, technology and training mixed with Israeli intelligence and combined arms competence.

The result was a rout. Pure and simple. A rout so thorough it surprised and, honestly, disturbed a lot of people.

So, now, as we assess the physical

battle space what comes next? Trump has put the US in the driver's seat to starve out Europe by keeping oil prices high. America's NATO allies still think this is a game of consensus politics where the optics of them refusing to assist Trump in opening the straits makes him look like a madman. In reality, it further clarifies the argument about them he's been making for years, that they are freeloading off America's security guarantees.

Do the women in the EU believe they can stand on their own without the US if it pulls out of NATO? NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte seems to be the only one among the Davos set that still has functional brain cells. And this, in turn, opens up the board for Russia's Vladimir Putin to get the peace deal he's been trying to get for more than a year with Ukraine. Putin has the whip hand over Europe who say they won't buy any Russian energy out of one side of their mouth while buying more LNG in 2025 than they did in 2024.

Russia's silence other than a few sternly worded statements by Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov, who is looking decreasingly relevant as the days go on, Russia's response has been nonexistent.

These are material benefits for the US as these events ultimately serve the following outcomes in the short and long term. First is that the US can become a more important energy exporter than it already is. Why wouldn't Trump, the transactional president, not see this set of circumstances to make the US richer. Money for the US and Donald Trump go together like chocolate and peanut butter.

This also gives him the leverage to address the weird patchwork of regional gasoline and diesel prices in the US and break wide open the political discussion of increasing not just refining capacity but completely overhauling the US energy distribution network.

In turn, this creates even more incentive to fix the oil output in Venezuela, opens opportunities in the Gulf of America, and

> accelerates the demise of the regime in Cuba, already well underway.

These points assist his addressing the cost of maintaining the physical chokepoints to the world economy by the threats they represent clear to billions of people around the world.

This is why Trump has been able to get so many countries to sign up to his Board of Peace for Gaza and create the Abraham Accords. He's making the case that America wants to work with any willing country to develop their advantages rather than pay the chaos premium of the previous status quo.

And that, most importantly, includes our supposed enemies like Iran, Russia, and China.

All these things then can speak directly to Americans frustrated and angry at Congress stopping him from getting nothing done

domestically. All we have to do to make great changes happen quickly is if a segment of our government and military act with clarity and purpose, thereby almost shaming Congress into passing the legislation they voted for.

Trump will be able to campaign for the midterms on all these points, offsetting the fatigue of Congress' obstinance.

But the biggest benefit to everyone is that chaos premium I just invoked above. Blowing out the Iran we've known for the past 47 years is just one more board clearing action of Davos' overly complicated geopolitical game board. Trump sent Peter Navarro out recently to lay this case out in public with an article published in the War Street Journal where he outlines trillions in costs we all have to bear for the potential chaos stemming from future geopolitical risks, such as Iran closing the straits, China invading Taiwan, violence between India and Pakistan, Pakistan and Afghanistan, mass migration, drug trafficking, etc.

![](_page_4_Picture_24.jpeg)

heading_path: THE PROS AND CONS OF WARMAKING
text:
#### (Warmaking, con't.)

All these things have a genesis block. These perma-conflicts are the true "Forever Wars" that Trump ran to not start and, explicitly end as many as he could. Getting countries to make peace, to stop having their local grievances nudged into violence by some outside actor from a western intelligence agency, lowers that risk premium we all pay at the gas pump, every April 15th in the US, or by burying a loved one after a terrorist attack or drug overdose.

Those "globalists" that Alex Jones used to care about exposing, are the ones running this freak show, they get the vig off these operations, making the decisions the rest of us must live with. And personally, I'm sick to death of it. If I have to be blackmailed to pay my taxes every year, I want to do so for reasons other than because a bunch of belligerent Persians who because they believe it is right to lie to the infidel, think it is their solemn right to threaten me with nuclear fire.

How about? No. How about? Go Fuck Yourself.

And when they ask, like the venal little pricks they actually are, what gives you the right to say they can't do that.

Donald Trump's answer, and that of most of the world is simply, because fuck you, that's why. I hear they're convening a meeting at the UN to discuss whether we're allowed to say that to them.

![](_page_5_Picture_6.jpeg)

![](_page_6_Picture_0.jpeg)
# DRAGGING THE CURSOR TO DOOMSDAY

### by Dexter K. White

Paul Ehrlich died this month. He was the author of *The Population Bomb*, a relic of the age of expert-driven public hysteria. Back when we had experts, at least ones we could believe in, even if they were full of crap like Ehrlich. I'm talking more about the expert archetype, the idea that some people actually knew what they were talking about.

The idea of the expert was based on academic authority, which still had its drawbacks. At the time, we trusted academics to present facts honestly, and debate the conclusions they drew from those facts with other experts. Seems quaint, right? The idea of honest scientific discourse had not yet disappeared.

Some of his critics characterized Ehrlich as a Malthusian, which was on point not just because of area of study. For those with a scientific background, or even a business modeling background, Malthus is exemplary of a style of projection that lacks any tamping of the fundamental assumption. In other words, the premise grows and grows.

The 1960s had a Chicken Little coterie of similar experts. Rachel Carson's *Silent Spring*, Ehrlich's *The Population Bomb* both guided the reader towards what could only be described as eventual cataclysm. I wonder how much more effective they might've been had they just been willing to say something like "unrestrained human population growth is probably gonna be unpleasant." Instead everything was always characterized as a race to the apocalypse.

As time progressed, a tendency towards histrionics amongst scientific academics only grew. This is kind of the essence of the brand now. Whether it's global warming, climate change, acid rain, deforestation, or the spotted owl, we've long lost the ability to frame a problem within a solvable context without devolving into hysteria.

From there, we arrived in the post-facts hellscape we know today. It's not that facts don't exist, it's that people can't agree on what they are, or grasp the implications of what happens when we don't.

### *Going Down the Mountain*

The carnival barker ethos took over the rational world and now few things are left to hearken back to the very different public discourse we used to have. Not that there weren't disingenuous thumbs on the scale at the time. Walter Cronkite and other CIA-managed zeitgeist sculptors were already on the case. Paul Ehrlich even wound up on *The Tonight Show*. But there is not necessarily a connection between *The Population Bomb* and the Club of Rome.

The goal of activists then was to build awareness and promote their solution. Whether or not we fully accepted the argument, or the validity of the facts, it was at least a rational process.

It was possible to be politically conservative, believe in ecology and still disagree with the "environmental" arguments leftist activists presented. There was still debate about how to achieve the generally agreed-upon goal to protect the natural environment. In fact, at the time, there were more conservatives working to save the environment while the discipline was still called "wildlife conservation."

Ehrlich's models may have lacked variables to prevent runaway exponential growth but it wasn't malice. However, the exponential growth story attracted the technocrat set. They recognized that terrorizing with science, which the average person knew they lacked aptitude in, was an ideal way to usher in a socialist bureaucracy worldwide. Not much has changed when it comes to this dynamic. Science is the fig leaf for those who crave only power.

Of course, as time progressed many started to realize that not only was the argument behind global warming disingenuous, but that it existed solely to mire honest people in pointless argument. That was because the people behind the global warming hysteria had no interest in the environment at all, but merely hijacked a well-intentioned movement to advance global collectivism. They merely wanted to harm the market economies to even the playing field between industrial capitalism and market socialism.

Absurdly, I'm starting to feel nostalgic about that time, but only because we have slipped into something even worse.

What's changed? Now, there is no pretense of academic authority, nor shared experience. There is not even an effort to ensure we are all working from the same set of facts, even if our conclusions are obviously different.

Instead we live in a screaming panopticon where all that matters is what color jersey you have on. At least the politics in *Idiocracy* were reduced to tractor pulls, where you were expected to be drunk.

### *Iran Redux*

So now we find ourselves in this Iran kerfuffle.

You know I could ramble on about how much propaganda is out there and how little is ultimately knowable as fact. But that's not even interesting. You either get it or you don't.

It seemed obvious from the start that the point of the whole exercise was to control the marginal barrel of oil. Not necessarily the price, but the supply. When looking back at the Venezuela operation, Tom and I quickly surmised it was as much about China's access to cheap bunker oil, as it was about Maduro. The Iran gambit follows this same through-line.

#### *(Doomsday, con't.)*

Whatever you think of the timing, the recklessness, the betrayal of campaign promises, this is about starving China's access to free-flowing oil.

For this to not be a massive mistake, the US needs to control the flow of Iranian oil when the shooting stops. This could be by occupying Kharg Island, or perhaps by securing the choke point at the Straits on Qeshm Island, but either way there will likely be an incursion by the US Marines before this is over.

Trump has bet his presidency on this and success is not assured. I do, however, think it is more likely than not. Mostly because Trump has no delusions of nation building. If you want to bet against his willingness to break stuff and leave, be my guest.

The Europeans are already playing the lame duck lottery, believing Trump will lose the midterms and be neutralized by endless investigations and pointless attempts at impeachment. I think they will regret measuring the drapes this early, but we will see.

### *A Change of Season*

I wrote in [Issue #94](https://tomluongo.me/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Gold-Goats-n-Guns-Issue-94-June-2025.pdf) last June, after the bombing of Iran's nuclear facilities, that the important takeaway was understanding it was about a change of the status quo.

People still can't see it. Consider the reflexive apoplexy we see now. So much of the punditry, amongst their many other absurdities, repeatedly put forward the notion that Iran has a right to close the Straits of Hormuz. Their Stockholm syndrome runs so deep, they cannot process basic facts. Some of it seems like anxiety under uncertainty even more than political bias.

Iran is a signatory to the [United Nations](https://www.un.org/depts/los/convention_agreements/texts/unclos/unclos_e.pdf)  [Convention on the Law of the Sea](https://www.un.org/depts/los/convention_agreements/texts/unclos/unclos_e.pdf)  [\(UNCLOS\)](https://www.un.org/depts/los/convention_agreements/texts/unclos/unclos_e.pdf), even if it has not fully ratified it.

Nonetheless, they recognize the midpoint between their coast and Oman's as the legal delineation between their territorial waters. Leave aside that the actual shipping lanes are almost entirely on the Omani side. Under agreed maritime law, nations are obliged to offer the right of innocent passage through waterways lest they reduce themselves to the status of pirates. It really is that simple. The fact that the world has put up with Iran's incessant threats against international commerce for as long as it has boggles the mind.

Now let's talk about the fact that the shipping traffic isn't even in Iranian water. The idea that Iran can charge a toll to pass through Oman's water is ridiculous, no matter how impressed the chattering classes are with "Persian sophistication." The Iranian parliament went so far as to pass a resolution to this effect. They might as well empower themselves to charge a toll to cross the Hudson River.

Shooting missiles at commercial vessels in another country's territorial water is nothing but an act of war. This has been Iran's perpetual threat to global energy flows since 1979. Allowing this condition to persist has been a complete failure of national sovereignty across the entire region and beyond.

Regardless of the questionable timing of the current conflict, the idea we should continue to tolerate this absurdity, and validate the Iranian delusion they have a right to subject us to it, is peak liberal nonsense.

![](_page_7_Picture_14.jpeg)

If you want a condensed version of the rarified air the John Kerry types have lived in for years, just read through [this article](https://tinabrown.substack.com/p/is-the-fog-of-war-between-trumps) by the Whore of Babylon herself, Tina Brown.

There can be no peace between us. These people cannot even perceive reality. And I'm not talking about the Iranians.

### *The World Around Us*

Going forward, there are two intractable problems in the political space in the US. One boils down to irrationality where intent should be. The other is a poor understanding of causality, which confounds the ability to observe shared reality. I know that sounds pretentious, but let's take them one at a time.

First, we used to live in a world of intentional actors. They had a worldview and tried to spread it. They made causal arguments. When we disagreed, we

challenged the facts they used their to support their logical arguments.

Secondly, if you can't agree on facts, there is no causality. If facts are either unknown, or people can't agree on what they are, what hope is there of valid argument or discourse? None, because all you have is noise.

Which brings me to Tucker Carlson. Just kidding, that's a whole article to itself.

### *Failure is Not the Only Option*

The difference between a guy like Paul Ehrlich and your favorite doomer pundit is that Ehrlich did the work. Ehrlich didn't want people to starve, was probably a bit neurotic, didn't check his math carefully enough and maybe didn't really examine his politics. But he was motivated by a simple desire to warn people about a coming famine. In other words, he was a good person. Even if he was wrong.

I used to make fun of anti-vax hippie moms because they were obviously liberal kooks. What could people like that know about virology? Look, squirrel!

Anyway.

A lot can be said about the so-called civil war amongst right wing podcasters and pseudo-influencers. Everyone feels betrayed by Trump trampling on principles they don't really have.

They are all talking their book, backfitting the world they perceive, which is composed mostly of nonsense propaganda and literal fakery, onto their pre-existing worldview. It's obvious since this conflict with Iran began, that a great many people in this space are simpletons.

Nobody on the right seems capable of imagining the possibility of Trump pulling this off, even though it has been absolutely reckless.

And that's because they haven't checked their models, they can't do anything but believe in amplification. They can't imagine that other countries are sick of Iran's crap, enough to support Trump seeing this through.

They can't imagine a possible ending where we wind up trading with Iran instead of bombing it. They want to live in the status quo forever, and unlike Ehrlich, they don't even have good intentions behind them.

You do not have to like it. I don't like it. But when the time comes, you're not going to believe how good the pistachios are.

heading_path: The End of the Chaos Premium
text:
### The End of the Chaos Premium

The hallmark of a good intelligence operation is sowing enough confusion that one or two facts leave most observers scratching their heads. The hallmark of a great intelligence operation is one where an entire generation believes something that simply isn't true.

For the past year or so I've been banging on about the US regaining control over the pricing mechanisms of specific strategic commodities – gold, silver, industrial metals.

Since the beginning of Trump 2.0 we have watched an historic move in all these assets. And it's been both thrilling and profitable to experience. During the years while Trump was in exile, the Fed implemented SOFR to gain US domestic control over the global pricing of US dollars.

Always over the horizon, however, was the biggest of all commodities, oil. At some point, Trump was going to have to make a move on the oil markets. It started in Caracas in January. He detoured into Mexico to put the cartels in the primary silverproducing region on notice, and now he's blown up the biggest chokepoint in the world, the Strait of Hormuz.

Hormuz is a classic physical chokepoint that has been used as a Sword of Damocles over the entire global economy by Iran for decades. That threat, in my opinion, has been part of a global chaos premium the entire globe has had to factor into its risk pricing for doing business.

That chaos premium is a good source of revenue for anyone willing to underwrite insurance for this risk. Most of that underwriting is done within the square mile known today as City of London.

Combined with the inherently speculative nature of the Brent Crude futures contract, which itself is misleading because no oil is delivered versus it, London's control over oil is far more complete than it looks like at first glance.

Rather than reflecting real oil supply and demand, the Brent contract is used purely as a benchmark pricing index for oil producers and consumers to write actual oil delivery contracts against. It is a derivative of oil demand rather than oil demand itself.

And because of that it can be influenced by forces that have nothing directly to do with oil. In short, it can and is used as a weapon against anyone operating against the best interests of those that help set the price.

With that as a background controlling idea, Trump's war in Iran is evolving rapidly, but it looks like, as of this writing, Trump is in complete control over the situation in the Straits. I'm going to walk through the timeline of events here and then speculate on where we wind up in the coming months.

- 1) February 28th, Trump begins Operation Epic Fury with Israel. The early reports are devastating for Iran as the combined operations hit hundreds of targets relentlessly. Ayatollah Khamenei and many senior IRGC commanders are killed.
- 2) March 1st and 2nd Iran immediately threatens to close the Strait of Hormuz, while OEF continues unabated. Iran's missile launches are following the same path as last June's "12 Day War," dropping by 40-50% day-over-day.
- 3) March 2nd the British shipping insurance alliance led by Lloyd's of London cancels shipping insurance on many voyages in process, raising the premiums 400-500% on others.

![](_page_8_Picture_15.jpeg)

5)That week, oil prices spike, dump, and begin rising again towards \$100+ / bbl Brent Crude. The spread between West Texas Intermediate (WTI) and Brent briefly closes then moves back to its pre-war \$4-5 Brent premium.

6)March 6th-7th Further details about Trump insurance plans emerge. Policies will be written by Chubb Ltd. (NYSE:CB) starting on 3/11/26 with a formal \$20 billion maritime reinsurance pool announced.

7)Ship traffic across Hormuz drops to nearly zero. Fog of war is high, with some ships making the run with

their transponders off.

- 8) Iran tries to negotiate over the straits closure in public with mixed messaging coming from the civilian government versus the IRGC. Uncertainty rules. Stock markets in freefall. Gold and silver top.
- 9) On March 11th- Iran "opens" Hormuz to ships of countries not allied with the US and Israel. Europe refuses to assist Trump escort ships through the chokepoint.
- 10) On March 14th, the US announces that a trio of ships carrying a Marine Expeditionary Unit left Japan on its way to the conflict. Speculation as to what their mission is commences. Brent/WTI spread blows out to \$10+ per barrel as energy prices explode. Natural gas prices in Europe explode, doubling on the Dutch TTF contract.
- 11) Trump publicly calls out NATO allies for not assisting. Setting the narrative hook that the alliance itself is a sham. France and Spain close their airspaces to US planes flying out of the UK towards the conflict.

![](_page_8_Picture_24.jpeg)

- 12) Ships begin queueing up to pay Iran's toll to transit the strait, confirmed by AIS tracking, by stopping at checkpoints at Qeshm island in the strait itself.
- 13) March 17th, Joe Kent, Director of the National Counterterrorism Center under the ODNI, resigns publicly citing reasons of conscience openly undermining Trump's rational for the war. It is revealed that Kent is under investigation for leaking classified materials by the FBI. Trump tweets out a map of the Strait with it renamed "Strait of America."
- 14) March 18th, ODNI Director Tulsi Gabbard gives her annual threat assessment testimony to the Senate. Kent goes on Tucker Carlson to feed the political narrative that Israel dragged Trump into this war.
- 15) March 19th, Trump secures commitments to cover Hormuz from major European partners, hosts Japan's PM Takaishi at the White House who affirms her support for his policy. Brent/WTI spread blows out to \$19 per barrel. Israel attacks Iranian oil refinery drawing everyone's ire, including Trump's.
- 16) Trump floats the threat that in order to receive US Navy protection in the Persian Gulf companies would also have to sign up for the US's insurance program. Oil prices begin stalling out. Brent/WTI spread closes in NY below \$14/ bbl.
- 17) Gold and silver prices roll over hard as reports flood in that the UAE is selling gold to defend its currency peg against the dollar.

If you look this over, you don't see a guy not in control of the escalatory framework at play. Instead, you get a picture of someone who undertook a very risky but necessary endeavor to remove not only a major node in the "chaos network" the old colonial powers of Europe profited from, particularly the UK, but also shift the US Navy's role as guarantor of global shipping from a cost center to a potential revenue stream.

Iran, in this analysis, is something that was an integral part of global chaos, not just from the typical Fox News talking points, but from a much deeper, far older framework, a business model that thrived on bureaucratic institutions, perpetual tit-for-tat geopolitical entanglements meant to ensure that the status quo was forever maintained.

Upward pressure on UK gilt yields and the Brent/WTI spread were the two biggest market signals I noticed which supported my analysis in real time **(see chart)**, as communicated during the Market Reports. The widening of the US/UK 10-year yield spread told me that Trump's targeting the London insurance market was having the intended impact.

The Brent/WTI spread told you that Trump would let the rest of the world feel the pain of his actions and force them to deal with his new reality. And that reality is that the US is now not only the guarantor of the price of oil in the physical sense but also that it will rewrite the ledgers of who profits from its flow.

The short-term pain of disruption is now the transition energy needed to reorganize not only oil flow, by bringing duplicitous gulf states into line, but also end the long-term threat of further regional chaos.

This short-term chaos isn't over. There are a lot of aftershocks that are going to occur in the coming weeks and months. But the benefits of removing the chaos premium from world trade and local development increases the probability of a much more prosperous world on the other side of this nexus point. That's what I'm seeing here.

For now, stay in cash as much as possible. I get the feeling Trump is willing to inflict more pain on his adversaries in NATO to get his desired geopolitical outcome, a new regime in Iran the world can work with and an end to the Russia/Ukraine conflict. If you have profits you can take off the table, do so.

There are still a ton of things up in the air that must resolve themselves to get to that point, and many of them will now focus on US domestic issues as well as instability in major European countries. A lot of business models break at \$100 per barrel oil. The dollar markets are tight.

The signal to go back in will be when oil prices correct significantly from where they are, money will begin flowing again. Equity markets will begin rallying. The Dow Jones Transportation Average has only corrected back to the breakout level, near 18000. This is a non-confirmation of the Dow Jones Industrials correcting hard into the war.

This non-confirmation tells us that Trump's calculus the US can handle what he's done has merit. For now, chaos reigns.

![](_page_10_Picture_27.jpeg)
## BREAKING THE MONOLITH

### Why Mirrors Aren't Lenses

Stanley Kubrick understood better than nearly every other filmmaker in history the power of the single image. The monolith from *2001: A Space Odyssey* is his most iconic image in a filmography filled with them. Kubrick began his career as a still photographer, one could argue he never stopped being one.

The last time I watched *2001* this became readily apparent. So much of that movie is still photography. In a film which refuses to hold your hand, his lens lingers, showing you the story rather than telling it to you. Still life by still life.

For my entire life (released the year I was born), that story has been endlessly debated, its meaning left to interpretation. More than any other movie I've ever watched, *2001* is like a stroll through an art gallery,

holding up a mirror to our biases, desires, faults, and strengths.

The geometrically and mathematically perfect monolith (1:4:9 dimensionally) is the exact opposite of a mirror. A black body that absorbs all light and reflects back our deepest fears.

It is singular in its imagery, but infinite in its implications. In that way, the monolith is a lens.

Why *2001* is so unnerving is because we want to be told what Kubrick thinks of his story. That's usually the point of filmmaking. How the director frames the shot, where they put the objects

relative to the camera is supposed to tell you what the artist thinks of the moment captured.

Kubrick's film is as much a commentary about that as it is a commentary on humanity's "evolution." It's nearly 100% observation.

When we step back and examine our reactions to current events, we must be aware of all these framing techniques. Who is drawing our attention to what and where? Why would they do that? What response are they trying to achieve?

In the age of social media those voices are everywhere. Our tweets, Instagrams, and TikToks were supposed to give us voices. What they've done is reverted us into response monkeys, bereft of thought. Worse, in the age of AI, images themselves have no veracity anymore. The camera's honesty that was supposed to make us into documentarians and citizen journalists has been supplanted in less than a generation, making us all doubt everything we see online.

Instead of complexity and nuance, we have narcissism. Instead of quiet reflection discussing the ambiguity of our limited knowledge, we have doomscrolling. Look, I'm not going to hold myself up on a pedestal on this issue, I'm as guilty as anyone.

During crisis moments, when a man as big as Trump acts to challenge the status quo at its highest level, nuance gets jettisoned and it's all fear, all the time.

And fear is reductionist. Reductionism leads to possession, not reason. We think we're looking into the mirror during these times, but we're really looking at the monolith.

This is how a situation, a history as deep and complex as Iran's conflict with Israel and the US, becomes nothing more than a mirror of our biases, fears, old wounds, and egos. Instead of seeing "Iran" as a patchwork of different

factions, verticals, cultures, and agendas, the warmongering neocons want us to believe they are just crazy Shia lunatics.

That leads to the people who hate the neocons and the Likudniks (with very good reason) to then accept everything Iran says at face value, and cheer on their blackmail and terrorism. They make the reductionist argument that because Israel is evil, Iran's response is justified.

When asked about Iran's options at this point, I respond with a simple question, "Which Iran?" Similarly, we should be asking when Israel does something,

"Which Israel?" Countries aren't monoliths. They aren't abysses of our fears. But someone really wants us to react this way.

It's high time we recognize the mechanics of this operation, because if we don't overcome that programming we'll never break through the reductionism.

We'll stare at the monolith for 58 more years and still be confused.

We're living through *2001's* immense second act, where everyman astronaut Dave Bowman is locked in a desperate struggle with an AI programmed with the typical fears of men with power, confronted with a vaster one, those who built the monolith.

They don't understand it and have trusted their agent, HAL-9000, to make sure the mission to study it doesn't fail because of human error. Bowman's triumph is overcoming all these obstacles.

His reward is becoming one with the monolith, living out the rest of his life, and being reborn.
